Three-Lens Framework
Framework Risks Mitigations Assess Resources Download XLSX

The Three-Lens Framework

From 36 risks and 15 mitigations (2022) to 87 risks and 44 mitigations (2026). Built on an MIT thesis. Extended with AIRI, OWASP, NIST, EU AI Act.

What Changed (2022 → 2026)

The original framework identified 36 risks and 15 mitigation techniques from a systematic review of 77 papers. Since May 2022, four shifts have reshaped the risk landscape:

1. LLMs Collapse the RPA/ML Distinction

ChatGPT (Nov 2022) and subsequent models handle both deterministic instruction-following and probabilistic reasoning in a single system. The entire RPA+ML architecture is being replaced by a single agent architecture.

2. AI Agents Operate Autonomously

Agentic systems plan, invoke tools, and execute multi-step action chains with reduced human involvement — a qualitative shift from deterministic automation to autonomous cognitive work.

3. Regulation Is Now Binding

EU AI Act (Aug 2024), NIST AI RMF 1.0 (Jan 2023) and GenAI Profile (Jul 2024), ISO/IEC 42001:2023. When the thesis was written, AI regulation was aspirational. Today it carries legal obligations with enforcement mechanisms and penalties.

4. The Risk Landscape Expanded by Orders of Magnitude

The MIT AI Risk Repository catalogs 1,700+ risks from 43 taxonomies with 1,366 real-world incidents. This v3 framework identifies 87 risks across 11 categories.

Why Three Lenses

The original two-axis framework was correct for its purpose — mapping risks the deploying enterprise controls. But agentic AI introduces risks that operate above the individual firm level. The third lens acknowledges what one firm can't solve alone.

🔧 Operational

Day-to-day execution. Engineering and security risks the firm directly controls. 36 risks across 4 categories.

🏛 Organizational

People, process, governance. Risks the firm controls through policy, training, contracts. 32 risks across 4 categories.

🌐 Systemic

Above-firm, societal-scale. Risks the firm influences but can't unilaterally mitigate. 19 risks across 3 categories. Requires collective action.

How It Compares

DimensionThree-LensOWASP LLMOWASP AgenticNIST AI RMFEU AI Act
Technical agent security✓✓✓◐—
Model safety & alignment✓◐◐◐◐
Workforce & human impact✓——◐◐
Governance & compliance✓——✓✓
Supply chain✓◐—◐◐
Systemic & societal risks✓——◐◐
Deployment sequencing✓————
Cross-axis interactions✓————

OWASP covers technical threats. NIST provides governance. The EU AI Act defines obligations. The Three-Lens Framework is the only one designed as a deployment readiness assessment — with a sequenced deployment path, severity tiers, and cross-axis interaction mapping.

Looking Forward (2028)

Five risks most likely to escalate based on deployment trajectories and incident data:

  1. Agent-Enabled Fraud at Scale — 353 incidents and counting. Fraud-as-a-service is emerging.
  2. Multi-Agent Emergent Behaviour — The gap between individual testing and multi-agent interaction is widening.
  3. AI Misinformation at Scale — 174 incidents. The cumulative effect on trust is not yet priced into risk models.
  4. Dangerous Dual-Use Proliferation — 77 entries. The gap between legitimate use and harmful repurposing is shrinking.
  5. Systemic Labor Market Restructuring — A slow-burning crisis that will reshape workforce strategy.
Three-Lens Framework

An AI agent risk assessment framework for enterprise deployments. 87 risks across 11 categories. Grounded in 1,366 real-world incidents.

Based On

Man, J. (2022). Towards the Future of Work: Managing the Risks of AI and Automation. MIT Sloan School of Management.

Extended with MIT AIRI, OWASP LLM & Agentic Top 10, NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001, and 27+ academic publications.

One lens isn't enough.
